title: Thai Numbers 1–8: Say Any Price, Phone Number, or Clock Face

vocab.explanation: หนึ่ง means 'one'. You'll use it constantly — for ordering one item, saying it's one o'clock, or reading a price that starts with one. One thing worth knowing: after a classifier, Thais sometimes say เดียว instead of หนึ่ง — like กาแฟแก้วเดียว — but หนึ่ง always works and is the safer choice when you're starting out.

exampleSentences.thai: ขอน้ำหนึ่งแก้วค่ะ
exampleSentences.english: Could I have one glass of water, please?
exampleSentences.note: หนึ่ง comes after the noun น้ำ and before the classifier แก้ว. That's the Thai word order: thing, then number, then classifier — the opposite of English.

vocab.explanation: สอง means 'two' and has a rising tone — your voice goes slightly up at the end. You'll use สอง for everything from two coffees to the second floor. You'll also hear it in สองโมง — two o'clock — and สองร้อย — two hundred baht. Very common in daily Thai.

exampleSentences.thai: มีลูกสองคนค่ะ
exampleSentences.note: สอง comes before the classifier คน, which is the classifier for people. Number always comes before the classifier in Thai.

vocab.explanation: สาม means 'three' — rising tone, same shape as สอง. Here's a fun one: สาม shows up in everyday Thai place names. สามแยก means a T-junction — literally 'three-split'. So if someone gives you directions and says สามแยก, they mean turn at the T-junction. The number three is hiding in Thai geography.

exampleSentences.thai: รอสามนาทีนะคะ
exampleSentences.note: สาม comes right before นาที. The pattern รอ + number + นาที is very reusable — swap in any number you know.

vocab.explanation: สี่ means 'four' and has a low tone — your voice stays flat and drops. Watch out for a near-homophone: สี with mid tone means 'colour'. สี่ with the low tone is the number, สี with mid tone is colour. In real conversation context makes it obvious, but it's good to know the difference exists.

exampleSentences.thai: สั่งข้าวสี่จานค่ะ
exampleSentences.note: จาน is the classifier for plates and flat dishes. สี่ comes before the classifier, after the noun — that's the same noun-number-classifier pattern you've seen before.

vocab.explanation: ห้า means 'five' and has a falling tone — your voice drops from high to low. Here's the most fun fact in this whole episode: in Thai internet slang, 555 means 'hahaha'. That's because ห้า sounds like 'haa' — so 555 is 'haa haa haa'. If a Thai person sends you 555 in a message, they're laughing. You'll see it constantly in Thai comments and chat.

exampleSentences.thai: รอห้านาทีนะครับ
exampleSentences.note: Same pattern as the three-minutes example. Once you know the numbers, you can drop any of them into รอ + number + นาที. Very flexible.

vocab.explanation: หก means 'six' — low tone, short and punchy. One thing worth knowing: หก is also a Thai verb meaning 'to fall' or 'to spill'. หกล้ม means 'to fall down', หกคะเมน means 'to somersault'. The number and the verb are pronounced the same way. You'll always know from context which meaning is intended — numbers appear near other numbers, verbs appear in action contexts.

exampleSentences.thai: หกโมงเย็นครับ
exampleSentences.note: โมง means o'clock and เย็น means evening. หกโมงเย็น is 6 PM. You can use this number-plus-โมง pattern with any of today's numbers to say what time it is.

vocab.explanation: เจ็ด means 'seven' — low tone. This one is very consistent and has no tricky homophone. เจ็ดวัน means 'seven days', which is also one week. เจ็ดโมง is seven o'clock. You'll use this one in phone numbers, prices, and times all the time. Nothing tricky here — just learn the sound and you're set.

exampleSentences.thai: อาทิตย์หนึ่งมีเจ็ดวันค่ะ
exampleSentences.translit: aa-thít nùeng mii jèt wan khâ
exampleSentences.note: อาทิตย์ means both 'week' and 'Sunday' in Thai. มี means 'has'. เจ็ดวัน is 'seven days'. The sentence literally says 'one week has seven days'.

vocab.explanation: แปด means 'eight' — low tone. Eight is considered lucky in Chinese culture, and that influence runs deep in Thailand, especially in Chinese-Thai communities and markets. You'll sometimes see prices ending in 8 at Thai-Chinese shops, and phone numbers loaded with eights are sold at a premium. แปดโมงเช้า is eight in the morning.

exampleSentences.thai: ตื่นแปดโมงเช้าค่ะ
exampleSentences.english: Wake up at eight in the morning.
exampleSentences.note: ตื่น means 'to wake up'. แปดโมงเช้า is 8 AM — แปด is eight, โมง is o'clock, เช้า is morning. You can already say what time you wake up in Thai.

blocks.speakerNote: A = tourist trying to give their phone number digit by digit in Thai. B = Thai shop staff with a notepad and pen. Visual cue: A wears a bright tourist cap on the left side of frame and speaks with hesitation; B holds a notepad on the right side with cooler, more professional lighting and a quick confident delivery. Voice: A is slower and uncertain; B is friendly but brisk. This block is the opening social clip — record it as a continuous take that can be lifted as a 30-second short.

lines.english: At a Thai phone shop. A tourist needs to register their number.
lines.speaker: narrator

lines.thai: สวัสดีค่ะ ขอเบอร์โทรหน่อยได้ไหมคะ
lines.translit: sà-wàt-dii khâ khǎaw booe-thoo nàwy dâi mǎi khá
lines.english: Hi! Could I have your phone number?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: ได้เลยครับ หนึ่ง สาม ห้า
lines.translit: dâi looei khráp nùeng sǎam hâa
lines.english: Sure! One, three, five...
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: ค่ะ แล้วต่อไปคะ
lines.english: Got it. And then?
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: เดี๋ยวนะครับ สี่ หรือสี่สิบ
lines.translit: dǐiaw ná khráp sìi rǔu sìi-sìp
lines.english: Wait... four? Or forty?
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: เบอร์โทรไม่มีสี่สิบค่ะ
lines.english: Phone numbers don't have forty.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: โอ้ สี่ครับ แล้วก็แปด สอง เจ็ด
lines.translit: ôo sìi khráp láaew gâaw pàaet sǎawng jèt
lines.english: Oh! Four. Then eight, two, seven.
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: เก้าหลักแล้วนะคะ ขาดอีกหนึ่งหลักค่ะ
lines.translit: kâo làk láaew ná khá khàat ìik nùeng làk khâ
lines.english: That's nine digits. You're still one short.
lines.speaker: B

lines.thai: อ๋อ หก
lines.english: Oh! Six!
lines.speaker: A

lines.thai: เดี๋ยวโทรให้นะคะ
lines.english: I'll call you in a moment!
lines.speaker: B

blocks.speakerNote: Cut from sketch to Nine in teacher mode, warm and direct. Three alternative cultural-context openers: (1) STATISTIC: 'Thai mobile numbers are always 10 digits, and every single one starts with 06, 08, or 09. So the first digit is always predictable — the challenge is everything after it.' (2) LINGUISTIC PUZZLE: 'Thailand has its own number symbols — ๑ ๒ ๓ — and you will see them on temple markers, royal documents, and official signs all over the country. But in everyday Thai life, most people use the same Arabic numerals as the rest of the world, so you do not need to learn the Thai symbols to function.' (3) COUNTERINTUITIVE: 'In Thai, the number five and the laugh sound are so close that 555 became the standard Thai way to type hahaha — and you will understand exactly why after this episode.' Default in lines below uses the Thai symbols angle.

lines.english: That sketch? She was trying to give her phone number digit by digit in Thai — and she kept second-guessing herself. By the end of this video, you'll be able to say every single digit in that conversation without hesitating.

Here's something interesting: Thailand actually has its own number symbols — ๑, ๒, ๓, ๔, ๕, ๖, ๗, ๘, ๙, ๑๐ — and you will see these on temple markers, royal documents, and some traditional menus all over the country. But in everyday life, most Thais use the same Arabic numerals as everywhere else. So you do not need to learn the Thai symbols to get by — but knowing they exist means you will not be surprised when you spot them on a temple door or a government sign.

Today I'm going to teach you the numbers one through eight in Thai. We'll go through each one, I'll explain what it means and how to use it, then you'll hear them all together in a real conversation, and finally we'll practise together. By the end of this video, you'll be able to say any single digit in Thai — which means you can give your phone number, read a simple price, and say what time it is.

Let's start with the vocabulary. I'm going to go through each number one by one. For each one, I'll say the Thai, explain what it means and anything interesting about it, and show you how to use it in a real sentence. Ready? Let's go.

lines.thai: หนึ่ง
lines.english: one

Our first number — หนึ่ง, nùeng. This means 'one'. Low falling tone — nùeng. You'll use this all the time: ordering one item, saying it's one o'clock, reading a price that starts with one. One thing worth knowing: after a classifier, Thais sometimes replace หนึ่ง with เดียว — so you might hear กาแฟแก้วเดียว instead of กาแฟหนึ่งแก้ว. Both mean one coffee. But หนึ่ง always works, especially when you're just starting out.

lines.thai: ขอน้ำหนึ่งแก้วค่ะ

lines.english: That means 'could I have one glass of water, please'. Notice the order: น้ำ — water — then หนึ่ง — one — then แก้ว — glass. In Thai, the number comes after the thing, not before it like in English. Thing, number, classifier. That's the pattern.

lines.thai: สอง
lines.english: two

สอง — sǎawng — means 'two'. Rising tone, so your voice goes slightly up at the end. You'll use สอง for anything that comes in twos: two coffees, two people, two days. You'll also hear it in สองโมง — two o'clock — and สองร้อย — two hundred baht. It shows up everywhere in daily Thai.

lines.thai: มีลูกสองคนค่ะ

lines.english: That means 'she has two children'. ลูก means child, สอง is two, and คน is the classifier for people. So: ลูก — สอง — คน. The number comes before the classifier. Same pattern as before.

lines.thai: สาม
lines.english: three

สาม — sǎam — means 'three'. Rising tone, same as สอง. Here's a fun one: สาม shows up in Thai place names. สามแยก literally means 'three splits' — and it's the word for a T-junction. So if someone gives you directions and says เลี้ยวที่สามแยก, they mean turn at the T-junction. The number three is hiding right there in everyday Thai geography.

lines.thai: รอสามนาทีนะคะ

lines.english: That means 'wait three minutes, okay?'. รอ is 'to wait', สาม is 'three', นาที is 'minutes', and นะ softens the sentence — it's a polite particle. The pattern รอ plus number plus นาที is very reusable. You can swap in any number you know.
four

สี่ — sìi — means 'four', low tone. Your voice stays flat and low. There's a near-homophone worth knowing: สี with mid tone — no tone mark — means 'colour'. สี่ with the low tone is the number four, สี with mid tone is colour. In real life context makes it obvious, but it's good to be aware the difference exists.

lines.thai: สั่งข้าวสี่จานค่ะ

lines.english: That means 'order four plates of rice'. สั่ง is 'to order', ข้าว is 'rice', สี่ is 'four', and จาน is the classifier for plates. Noun, number, classifier — you're getting good at spotting that pattern.

lines.thai: ห้า
lines.english: five

ห้า — hâa — means 'five', falling tone. Here is the most fun fact in this whole episode: in Thai internet slang, 555 means 'hahaha'. That's because ห้า sounds like 'haa' — so 555 is 'haa haa haa'. If a Thai person sends you 555 in a message, they're laughing. You will see it constantly in Thai comments, group chats, and social media. Now you're in on the joke.

lines.thai: รอห้านาทีนะครับ

lines.english: That's 'wait five minutes, okay?' — exactly the same structure as the three-minutes example. Once you know the numbers, you can drop any of them into that template. Very flexible.

lines.thai: หก
lines.english: six

หก — hòk — means 'six', low tone. One thing worth knowing: หก is also a Thai verb meaning 'to fall' or 'to spill'. หกล้ม means 'to fall down', and หกคะเมน means 'to somersault'. The number and the verb are pronounced the same way. You'll always know from context which one is meant — numbers appear near other numbers, verbs appear in action sentences.

lines.thai: หกโมงเย็นครับ

lines.english: That means 'six in the evening' — 6 PM. โมง is the Thai word for o'clock, and เย็น means evening. หกโมงเย็น — six o'clock evening — is 6 PM. You can use this number-plus-โมง pattern with any of today's numbers to say what time it is.

lines.thai: เจ็ด
lines.english: seven

เจ็ด — jèt — means 'seven', low tone. This one is very consistent — no tricky homophones, no tone surprises. เจ็ดวัน means 'seven days', which is also one week. เจ็ดโมง is seven o'clock. You will use this one in phone numbers, prices, and times all the time. Just learn the sound and you're done.

lines.thai: อาทิตย์หนึ่งมีเจ็ดวันค่ะ
lines.translit: aa-thít nùeng mii jèt wan khâ

lines.english: That means 'one week has seven days'. อาทิตย์ means week — and also Sunday, interestingly — มี means 'has', and เจ็ดวัน is 'seven days'. Two of today's numbers in one sentence: หนึ่ง and เจ็ด.

lines.thai: แปด
lines.english: eight

แปด — pàaet — means 'eight', low tone. Eight is considered lucky in Chinese culture, and that influence runs deep in Thailand — especially in Chinese-Thai communities and markets. Prices ending in 8 or phone numbers loaded with eights are genuinely popular in some Thai-Chinese businesses. แปดโมงเช้า is eight in the morning — 8 AM.

lines.thai: ตื่นแปดโมงเช้าค่ะ

lines.english: That means 'wake up at eight in the morning'. ตื่น is 'to wake up', แปด is 'eight', โมง is 'o'clock', and เช้า is 'morning'. You can already say what time you wake up in Thai.

OK, so now you know all eight numbers. What I'm going to do now is put them together into a short conversation — imagine you're at a small coffee stall in Thailand, asking about the time and the price. I'm going to speak at natural speed, the way a Thai person would actually talk. Don't pause the video. Just listen and see how much you can understand. Ready? Here we go.

lines.thai: กี่โมงแล้วคะ
สามโมงครับ
ขอบคุณค่ะ แล้วกาแฟหนึ่งแก้วเท่าไหร่คะ
มีห้าอย่างครับ ราคาต่างกัน
เอาอย่างที่สองค่ะ
สี่สิบบาทครับ
ขอบคุณค่ะ

lines.english: How was that? Could you catch some of those numbers? Don't worry if you didn't get everything — that's completely normal at this stage. Now let's break it down, sentence by sentence.
I'm going to go through that same conversation again, but this time sentence by sentence. I'll say the Thai, and then I'll explain exactly what each part means.

lines.thai: กี่โมงแล้วคะ

lines.english: What time is it?

กี่โมงแล้วคะ — what time is it? กี่ means 'how many', โมง means 'o'clock', and แล้ว adds a sense of 'already' or 'now'. Together they mean 'how many o'clock already?' — which is just the Thai way of asking the time. คะ at the end is the polite feminine particle. This is the phrase to memorise if you want to ask the time in Thai.

lines.thai: สามโมงครับ

lines.english: Three o'clock.

สามโมงครับ — three o'clock. สาม is three, โมง is o'clock, ครับ is the polite masculine particle. Notice how simple it is: just the number plus โมง. To say five o'clock, you say ห้าโมง. Seven o'clock is เจ็ดโมง. You can build all the o'clock phrases right now with what you've learned today.

lines.thai: ขอบคุณค่ะ แล้วกาแฟหนึ่งแก้วเท่าไหร่คะ
lines.translit: khàawp-khun khâ láaew gaa-faae nùeng gâaew thâo-rài khá
khàawp-khun khâ láaew gaa-faae nùeng gâaew thâo-rài khá

lines.english: Thank you. And how much is one coffee?

Two useful patterns here. First: กาแฟหนึ่งแก้ว — one coffee — which is noun-number-classifier. กาแฟ is coffee, หนึ่ง is one, แก้ว is the classifier for cups and glasses. Second: เท่าไหร่ means 'how much'. Drop เท่าไหร่ at the end of anything and you've turned it into a price question. That one word does a lot of work.

lines.thai: มีห้าอย่างครับ ราคาต่างกัน
lines.translit: mii hâa yàang khráp raa-khaa dtàang-gan
mii hâa yàang khráp raa-khaa dtàang-gan

lines.english: There are five kinds. The prices are different.

มี means 'there is', 'there are', or 'to have'. ห้าอย่าง is 'five kinds' — ห้า is five and อย่าง is a classifier meaning 'type' or 'kind'. ราคา means 'price', and ต่างกัน means 'different from each other'. The vendor is telling you: we have five types, and they each cost something different. Very natural Thai.

lines.thai: เอาอย่างที่สองค่ะ

lines.english: I'll take the second one.

เอา means 'I'll take' or 'I want'. อย่างที่สอง means 'the second one' — อย่าง is 'item or kind', ที่ is an ordinal marker, and สอง is two. So ที่สอง literally means 'at-two' — which is 'second'. You can use this with any number: ที่หนึ่ง is first, ที่สาม is third. That ที่-plus-number pattern is very handy.

lines.thai: สี่สิบบาทครับ

lines.english: Forty baht.

สี่สิบบาท — forty baht. สี่ is four, สิบ is ten, and in Thai you multiply: four times ten equals forty. สิบ itself means ten. With today's numbers, you can already say 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 baht just by combining what you know. บาท is the Thai currency. That's a huge range of prices from just eight numbers.

lines.thai: ขอบคุณค่ะ

lines.english: Thank you.
ขอบคุณค่ะ — thank you. ขอบคุณ is the core thank-you phrase, and ค่ะ is the polite feminine particle. You've seen this one before. It's a natural way to close a transaction and leave on a warm note.

Now it's your turn to practise. I'm going to say each sentence slowly, and I want you to repeat after me. Don't be shy — say it out loud! Speaking is how you learn. I'll leave a gap after each sentence for you to repeat.

lines.thai: กี่โมงแล้วคะ
lines.thaiSplit: กี่ โมง แล้ว คะ

lines.thai: สามโมงครับ
lines.thaiSplit: สาม โมง ครับ

lines.thai: ขอบคุณค่ะ แล้วกาแฟหนึ่งแก้วเท่าไหร่คะ
lines.thaiSplit: ขอบคุณ ค่ะ แล้ว กาแฟ หนึ่ง แก้ว เท่าไหร่ คะ
lines.translit: khàawp-khun khâ láaew gaa-faae nùeng gâaew thâo-rài khá

lines.thai: มีห้าอย่างครับ ราคาต่างกัน
lines.thaiSplit: มี ห้า อย่าง ครับ ราคา ต่างกัน
lines.translit: mii hâa yàang khráp raa-khaa dtàang-gan

lines.thai: เอาอย่างที่สองค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: เอา อย่าง ที่ สอง ค่ะ

lines.thai: สี่สิบบาทครับ
lines.thaiSplit: สี่สิบ บาท ครับ

lines.thai: ขอบคุณค่ะ
lines.thaiSplit: ขอบคุณ ค่ะ

lines.english: Great job! If that felt hard, that's totally fine — rewind and do it again. Repetition is how you get better at this.
OK, quiz time! I'm going to give you a situation in English, and you need to say the Thai phrase. I'll give you a few seconds to think, and then I'll give you the answer. Ready?

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Pause 3 seconds in silence after the cue line before answering.

lines.english: You're at a Thai market and the seller asks how many you want. You want five. How do you say the number five in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: ห้า

lines.english: That's right — ห้า means five. Falling tone — hâa. And remember, 555 in Thai texting means hahaha, because ห้า sounds like haa.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Pause 3 seconds after the cue.

lines.english: Someone asks you what time it is. It's three o'clock. How do you answer in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: สามโมงครับ

lines.english: That's right — สามโมงครับ means three o'clock. สาม is three, โมง is o'clock. Swap สาม for any of today's numbers and you can say a different time.

blocks.speakerNote: Look to camera. Pause 3-4 seconds after the cue.

lines.english: You're giving your phone number to someone and the digit you need to say is eight. How do you say it in Thai?
Try saying it now...

lines.thai: แปด

lines.english: That's right — แปด means eight. Low tone, pàaet. Lucky number in Thai-Chinese culture, so you will hear it a lot in prices and phone numbers.
Let's do a quick recap of everything you learned today.

Today you learned the numbers one through eight in Thai: หนึ่ง, สอง, สาม, สี่, ห้า, หก, เจ็ด, แปด. You also picked up how to ask the time — กี่โมงแล้ว — how to ask the price — เท่าไหร่ — and how Thai combines numbers to make prices like สี่สิบบาท. With these eight numbers, you can already give your phone number, say what time it is, and read simple prices in Thai.
Thanks for watching! If this helped you, give it a thumbs up and subscribe so you don't miss the next one.

blocks.speakerNote: Pre-Rev-6 — may need retro-fit when template updates.

lines.english: In the next episode, we're going to talk about food — how to order, how to say what you like, and how to tell someone a dish is delicious. Here's a phrase to get you thinking:

lines.thai: อร่อยมากเลย
lines.english: It's so delicious!

imagePrompts.subject: Thai coffee stall conversation scene

imagePrompts.prompt: Watercolour illustration of a calm Thai study scene — an open Thai language workbook on a teak wood table, a cup of hot tea beside it, soft diffused window light, Thai script visible on the open pages, horizontal composition
imagePrompts.subject: Thai language study and breakdown practice

imagePrompts.prompt: Flat illustration of a cheerful quiz card with a large question mark in bright yellow, small icons of a clock face and a price tag floating around it, Thai numeral symbols in the background, clean playful design, horizontal composition
imagePrompts.subject: Quiz and drill practice

imagePrompts.prompt: Watercolour illustration of a spread of Thai street food on a wooden table — pad thai in a takeaway box, green papaya salad, two coconut drinks — warm colours, blurred Thai night market lights in background, horizontal composition
imagePrompts.subject: Thai food spread for recap and next episode teaser
